title: Per capita electricity generation from fossil fuels
caption: In 2025, per capita electricity generation from fossil fuels varies widely across the globe, ranging from near zero to over 10,000 kilowatt-hours per person. The map displays a continuous color scale where the darkest shades represent the highest generation levels. The highest values appear in the Middle East, specifically Saudi Arabia and the United Arab Emirates, which fall into the darkest band exceeding 10,000 kWh. High generation levels, generally between 2,000 and 5,000 kWh, cover most of North America, Australia, Russia, and parts of East Asia including China and South Korea. Western Europe shows moderate levels, with countries like Germany and Poland falling in the 1,000 to 2,000 kWh range. In contrast, the lowest values, often below 200 kWh, dominate the African continent, particularly in sub-Saharan regions, as well as parts of South Asia and Central America. Large areas of Greenland and Antarctica are marked with hatching to indicate no data. The geographic distribution shows a concentration of high per capita fossil fuel electricity generation in industrialized economies and major oil-producing nations, while lower values cluster in developing regions across the Global South.
